news_summary: Breakthroughs in fossil research, quantum computing, protein imaging, and nuclear timekeeping showcase remarkable advancements driving innovation across science and technology.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans la recherche sur les fossiles, l'informatique quantique, l'imagerie des protéines et la chronométrie nucléaire témoignent des progrès remarquables qui stimulent l'innovation dans les domaines scientifiques et technologiques.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la investigación sobre fósiles, la computación cuántica, la obtención de imágenes de proteínas y la cronometría nuclear ponen de manifiesto los notables progresos que impulsan la innovación en el ámbito de la ciencia y la tecnología.

articles.title: The missing notebooks that solved a 55-million-year-old fossil mystery
articles.summary: A spectacular fossil fish discovered on a remote cliff in New Zealand nearly 30 years ago has finally revealed its full story thanks to an unexpected discovery: the original collector’s long-lost field notebooks. The 1.2-meter fossil, preserved in stunning three-dimensional detail, belonged to an ancient tarpon-like predator that cruised New Zealand waters about 55 million years ago.
articles.content:
Field notebooks recently recovered from a late paleontologist have provided the crucial missing details researchers needed to complete a study of a remarkable fossil fish discovered nearly three decades ago.

The story began in 1999 when Dr. Richard Köhler uncovered the fossil during a research expedition to Pitt Island in the Chatham Islands.

While exploring the island's western coastline above Waihere Bay, Köhler spotted a three-dimensionally preserved, mummified fish fossil embedded in a steep section of cliff that was almost impossible to reach.

To recover it, he walked 3km back to his lodging in Flowerpot Bay to borrow a ladder. He then returned to the site and carefully extracted the fossil in several large, extremely heavy blocks.

Once back in Dunedin, Köhler brought the specimen to the University of Otago's Department of Geology. Emeritus Professor Daphne Lee recalls that she and the late Professor Ewan Fordyce immediately recognized its significance.

"It was quite unlike any other fish fossil known from Aotearoa, New Zealand," Daphne says.

Remarkable Ancient Predator Identified

The fossil was painstakingly prepared by the late fossil preparator Andrew Grebneff and remained in the Department's collection for several years. Eventually, it attracted the attention of Professor Mike Gottfried, a fossil fish specialist from Michigan State University.

Mike had previously collaborated with Ewan on several studies involving fossil fishes and sharks from New Zealand, and he soon began investigating the unusual specimen.

Researchers determined that the 1.2m long mummified fish was a tarpon, a species no longer found in New Zealand waters.

Modern tarpon are large predatory fish that typically swallow smaller fish whole. The fossil's anatomy suggests it lived in much the same way. Its elongated body, thick rigid scales, powerful tail fin, and large upward-facing mouth all point to an active predator near the top of the food chain.

Missing Information Delays Research

Despite the importance of the fossil, researchers faced a major obstacle.

Critical geological information about the exact discovery site was missing, and Richard had passed away several years earlier.

By the time Ewan died in November 2023, a draft scientific paper had already been prepared. However, work on the study could not move forward because researchers lacked the detailed field information needed to properly document where and how the fossil had been found.

The breakthrough came in early 2025 when one of Richard's children, who was studying at Otago, visited the Department hoping to locate photographs of his father.

After meeting with Daphne, Richard's family decided to donate his field notebooks, including those from the original Pitt Island expedition.

"This enabled us to get enough specific locality information to prepare a Fossil Record Form and to scientifically catalogue the fossil," Daphne says.

New Fossil Species Named

The completed study was recently published in the New Zealand Journal of Geology and Geophysics.

According to the researchers, it represents the first report of a high-in-the-food-chain pursuit predatory bony fish from rocks of Paleogene age (around 55 million years ago) in Aotearoa.

The fossil was given the name Ikawaihere koehleri in honor of Richard Köhler and the location where it was discovered. The authors also thanked Heidi Lanauze and the Hokotehi Moriori Trust for approving the name.

Mike says it was a privilege to work on the "remarkable fossil."

"It greatly expands our knowledge of the evolutionary history of tarpons, and it preserves unique and unusual features in exquisite 3D detail," he says.

"It is certainly among the most important and impressive fossils recovered to date from Aotearoa, New Zealand."

Daphne says she is delighted that the study has finally reached completion.

"It is a fitting tribute to Richard, Ewan and Andrew. We're extremely grateful to Richard's family for donating his notebooks -- we could not have done this without them."
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in paleontology where the recovery of lost field notebooks enabled researchers to complete a study on a rare 55-million-year-old fossil fish. This discovery expands knowledge of evolutionary history, has broad scientific significance, and highlights the importance of preserving research data.
articles.personality_title: Lost notebooks help solve 55-million-year-old fossil mystery in New Zealand
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Nearly 30 years ago, a rare fossil fish was found on a remote cliff in New Zealand, but scientists lacked important details to fully understand it because the original collector’s notes were missing.

**What happened** – In 1999, Dr. Richard Köhler discovered a 1.2-meter fossil of an ancient tarpon-like predator on Pitt Island. The fossil was carefully removed and studied, but key information about where exactly it was found was lost after Dr. Köhler passed away. In early 2025, his family donated his long-lost field notebooks to researchers, providing the missing details needed to complete the study.

**Impact** – Thanks to the notebooks, scientists were able to officially name the fossil Ikawaihere koehleri and confirm it as a top predator fish that lived 55 million years ago. This discovery is unique because it is the first detailed report of such a predatory bony fish from that time in New Zealand, offering new insights into the history of tarpons and ancient marine life.

**What's next step** – The study has been published, and researchers hope the detailed fossil and its story will help further research on ancient fish and their environments. The notebooks also show how preserving research records can be crucial for future discoveries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The return of lost field notebooks allowed scientists to unlock the full story of a rare 55-million-year-old fossil fish, revealing new details about ancient life in New Zealand.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des carnets oubliés révèlent le mystère d’un fossile vieux de 55 millions d’années en Nouvelle-Zélande
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a près de 30 ans, un poisson fossile rare a été découvert sur une falaise isolée en Nouvelle-Zélande, mais les scientifiques manquaient d’informations importantes pour le comprendre car les carnets du chercheur avaient disparu.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 1999, le Dr Richard Köhler a trouvé un fossile de 1,2 mètre d’un ancien prédateur ressemblant à un tarpon sur l’île Pitt. Le fossile a été soigneusement extrait et étudié, mais des détails clés sur le lieu exact de la découverte ont été perdus après le décès du Dr Köhler. Début 2025, sa famille a donné ses carnets de terrain longtemps oubliés aux chercheurs, fournissant les informations manquantes pour compléter l’étude.

**Impact** – Grâce aux carnets, les scientifiques ont pu nommer officiellement le fossile Ikawaihere koehleri et confirmer qu’il s’agissait d’un poisson prédateur de premier rang ayant vécu il y a 55 millions d’années. Cette découverte est unique car c’est le premier rapport détaillé d’un tel poisson osseux prédateur de cette époque en Nouvelle-Zélande, offrant de nouvelles connaissances sur l’histoire des tarpons et de la vie marine ancienne.

**Prochaine étape** – L’étude a été publiée, et les chercheurs espèrent que ce fossile détaillé et son histoire aideront à mieux comprendre les poissons anciens et leurs environnements. Les carnets montrent aussi l’importance de conserver les archives de recherche pour de futures découvertes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le retour des carnets de terrain perdus a permis aux scientifiques de révéler toute l’histoire d’un poisson fossile rare vieux de 55 millions d’années, apportant de nouveaux détails sur la vie ancienne en Nouvelle-Zélande.
articles.personality_title_es: Cuadernos perdidos resuelven el misterio de un fósil de 55 millones de años en Nueva Zelanda
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace casi 30 años se encontró un raro pez fósil en un acantilado remoto de Nueva Zelanda, pero los científicos no tenían detalles importantes para entenderlo completamente porque faltaban las notas del investigador original.

**Qué pasó** – En 1999, el Dr. Richard Köhler descubrió un fósil de 1,2 metros de un antiguo depredador parecido a un tarpon en la isla Pitt. El fósil fue retirado con cuidado y estudiado, pero se perdió información clave sobre el lugar exacto del hallazgo tras la muerte del Dr. Köhler. A principios de 2025, su familia donó los cuadernos de campo perdidos, proporcionando los detalles necesarios para completar el estudio.

**Impacto** – Gracias a los cuadernos, los científicos pudieron nombrar oficialmente el fósil Ikawaihere koehleri y confirmar que era un pez depredador importante que vivió hace 55 millones de años. Este descubrimiento es único porque es el primer informe detallado de un pez óseo depredador de esa época en Nueva Zelanda, ofreciendo nueva información sobre la historia de los tarpones y la vida marina antigua.

**Próximo paso** – El estudio se ha publicado, y los investigadores esperan que el fósil detallado y su historia ayuden a futuras investigaciones sobre peces antiguos y sus ambientes. Los cuadernos también muestran la importancia de conservar los registros de investigación para descubrimientos futuros.

**Resumen en una frase** – La recuperación de los cuadernos de campo perdidos permitió a los científicos descubrir toda la historia de un raro fósil de pez de 55 millones de años, revelando nuevos detalles sobre la vida antigua en Nueva Zelanda.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a rugged coastal cliff at Pitt Island with a large, three-dimensional fossil fish embedded in the rock face, illuminated softly to highlight its intricate scales and powerful form, alongside an open vintage field notebook resting on a weathered wooden table nearby, symbolizing the recovered scientific knowledge that solved the ancient mystery.

articles.title: Brain-inspired chip runs near absolute zero and could transform quantum computing
articles.summary: Scientists at the University of Hong Kong have created a remarkable new type of brain-inspired chip that can function just above absolute zero, one of the coldest environments imaginable. By using a standard silicon carbide transistor in a completely new way, the team made a single device behave like an energy-efficient neuron, firing electrical “spikes” similar to those in the human brain.
articles.content:
Researchers at the University of Hong Kong (HKU) have unveiled a significant advance in cryogenic electronics that could help overcome key challenges in quantum computing and support future deep space missions. The team, from HKU's Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering and the Centre for Advanced Semiconductors and Integrated Circuits (CASIC), developed a programmable neuromorphic hardware platform capable of operating at temperatures near absolute zero.

The research was led by Professor Yuhao Zhang and PhD student Xin Yang. Their work introduces a new method for generating and controlling negative differential resistance (NDR) in industry standard Silicon Carbide (SiC) MOSFETs. Using this approach, the researchers demonstrated for the first time that a single transistor can reproduce the energy efficient "spiking" activity of biological neurons at temperatures as low as 10mK.

Brain-Inspired Hardware for Quantum Computing

Quantum computers depend on sophisticated control electronics to manage qubits, which are highly sensitive and must be kept at millikelvin temperatures. Existing silicon based control systems consume considerable power and produce unwanted heat, making it necessary to position them away from the qubits themselves. That distance creates extensive wiring requirements that can hinder performance and make large scale quantum computers more difficult to build.

"Our work introduces a hardware platform that can be integrated alongside quantum processors," said Professor Zhang. "By using the unique carrier dynamics in silicon carbide, we can create circuits that are thousands of times more energy-efficient than conventional electronics, significantly reducing the thermal load on cryogenic systems."

Silicon Carbide Reveals Unique Cryogenic Behavior

The team found that SiC MOSFETs display a strong "S-shape" NDR effect when cooled below 2K. This behavior is driven by electron-donor impact ionization (EDII). Unlike other technologies that depend on heat generated within a device, the newly observed mechanism arises directly from the material's atomic properties. As a result, it remains highly stable and can be reproduced consistently across different manufacturing batches.

"This is a robust and scalable approach," said Mr. Yang. "Because SiC is already used globally in electric vehicles and power grids, we can leverage existing industrial foundries to manufacture these cryogenic chips on 300-mm wafers."

From Artificial Neurons to Deep Space Missions

The study also demonstrated that these artificial neurons can be linked together, or "cascaded," into larger networks. This capability could enable advanced local data processing at cryogenic temperatures and improve important quantum computing functions such as quantum error correction and real time quantum control.

The potential applications extend well beyond quantum computing. Because the circuits are designed to operate reliably in extremely cold environments, they could also be valuable for deep space exploration. Future systems may be able to function in the harsh conditions found on the Moon's surface or in the distant regions of our solar system.

The findings were published in Nature Communications in a paper titled "Cryogenic neuromorphic circuits using gate-controlled negative differential resistance in silicon carbide."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in cryogenic neuromorphic hardware that can operate near absolute zero, enabling more energy-efficient quantum computing and potential applications in deep space missions. This advance addresses key challenges in quantum computing scalability and energy consumption, with broad implications for future technology and space exploration. The story is focused, detailed, and describes a tangible, impactful innovation.
articles.personality_title: New brain-inspired chip works near absolute zero to boost quantum computing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers need to work at extremely cold temperatures, close to absolute zero, to keep their tiny parts called qubits stable. However, the electronics used to control qubits usually produce heat and use a lot of power, which makes building bigger quantum computers difficult.

**What happened** – Scientists at the University of Hong Kong created a new chip inspired by how brain neurons work. This chip uses a common silicon carbide transistor in a new way, allowing it to fire electrical signals like neurons but at temperatures as low as 10 millikelvin (near absolute zero). This is the first time a single transistor has shown this energy-efficient “spiking” behavior at such cold temperatures.

**Impact** – This new chip can be placed close to quantum processors without adding much heat, reducing the need for long wires and making quantum computers easier to build and more efficient. The technology is stable, can be mass-produced using existing factories, and could also help computers work in very cold places like the Moon or deep space.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to connect many of these artificial neurons together to create larger networks. This could improve quantum error correction and control, making quantum computers more reliable. The technology might also be tested for space missions where electronics must survive extreme cold.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A brain-inspired silicon carbide chip working near absolute zero offers a new way to build energy-efficient quantum computers and devices for cold space environments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle puce inspirée du cerveau fonctionne près du zéro absolu pour améliorer l’informatique quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques doivent fonctionner à des températures extrêmement basses, proches du zéro absolu, pour garder leurs minuscules parties appelées qubits stables. Cependant, l’électronique utilisée pour contrôler les qubits produit souvent de la chaleur et consomme beaucoup d’énergie, ce qui complique la construction d’ordinateurs quantiques plus grands.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Hong Kong ont créé une nouvelle puce inspirée du fonctionnement des neurones du cerveau. Cette puce utilise un transistor en carbure de silicium d’une manière nouvelle, lui permettant d’envoyer des signaux électriques comme des neurones, mais à des températures aussi basses que 10 millikelvins (près du zéro absolu). C’est la première fois qu’un transistor unique montre ce comportement “à impulsions” économe en énergie à ces températures.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle puce peut être placée près des processeurs quantiques sans générer beaucoup de chaleur, réduisant ainsi le besoin de longs câbles et rendant les ordinateurs quantiques plus faciles à construire et plus efficaces. La technologie est stable, peut être produite en masse avec les usines existantes, et pourrait aussi aider les ordinateurs à fonctionner dans des endroits très froids comme la Lune ou l’espace profond.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de connecter plusieurs de ces neurones artificiels pour créer des réseaux plus grands. Cela pourrait améliorer la correction d’erreurs quantiques et le contrôle, rendant les ordinateurs quantiques plus fiables. La technologie pourrait aussi être testée pour des missions spatiales où l’électronique doit survivre à un froid extrême.

**En une phrase** – Une puce en carbure de silicium inspirée du cerveau fonctionnant près du zéro absolu offre une nouvelle façon de construire des ordinateurs quantiques économes en énergie et des appareils pour les environnements froids de l’espace.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo chip inspirado en el cerebro funciona cerca del cero absoluto para mejorar la computación cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas deben operar a temperaturas extremadamente bajas, cerca del cero absoluto, para mantener estables sus partes diminutas llamadas qubits. Sin embargo, la electrónica que controla los qubits suele generar calor y consumir mucha energía, lo que dificulta construir computadoras cuánticas más grandes.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Hong Kong crearon un nuevo chip inspirado en el funcionamiento de las neuronas del cerebro. Este chip usa un transistor de carburo de silicio de una forma nueva, permitiéndole enviar señales eléctricas como las neuronas, pero a temperaturas tan bajas como 10 milikelvin (cerca del cero absoluto). Es la primera vez que un solo transistor muestra esta actividad de “picos” eficiente en energía a tan bajas temperaturas.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo chip puede colocarse cerca de los procesadores cuánticos sin generar mucho calor, reduciendo la necesidad de cables largos y haciendo que las computadoras cuánticas sean más fáciles de construir y más eficientes. La tecnología es estable, puede producirse en masa con fábricas existentes, y también podría ayudar a que las computadoras funcionen en lugares muy fríos como la Luna o el espacio profundo.

**Qué sigue** – Los investigadores planean conectar muchos de estos neuronas artificiales para crear redes más grandes. Esto podría mejorar la corrección de errores cuánticos y el control, haciendo que las computadoras cuánticas sean más confiables. La tecnología también podría probarse para misiones espaciales donde la electrónica debe sobrevivir a un frío extremo.

**Una frase clave** – Un chip de carburo de silicio inspirado en el cerebro que funciona cerca del cero absoluto ofrece una nueva forma de construir computadoras cuánticas eficientes y dispositivos para ambientes fríos del espacio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricate silicon carbide crystal structure emitting soft pulses of light like neural spikes, set against a serene, starry deep space background with subtle hints of icy landscapes symbolizing near absolute zero temperatures and futuristic quantum circuits branching out like interconnected neurons.

articles.title: An innovative technology boosts image quality for protein structures
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 12 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01858-1After years of effort, two research teams have developed ‘laser phase plate’ systems that could help cryo-electron-microscopy users to generate high-quality structures for a broad range of proteins.
articles.content:
An engineer at Biohub in Redwood City, California, makes adjustments to the laser phase plate cavity inside a cryo-electron microscope.Credit: Biohub

For 15 years, structural biologists have debated the feasibility of a technology that could improve the resolution of protein structures produced by cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM). The technology, called a laser phase plate (LPP), should extend the cryo-EM technique to a broader range of proteins than was previously possible, and simplify tomography experiments that reconstruct protein behaviour in the cellular environment.

This month, the optimists were vindicated, with a publication on 11 June in Science1 and a preprint posted in bioRxiv on 5 June2 demonstrating two LPP designs that effectively boost the image quality of small proteins in cryo-EM experiments.

“It’s the first exciting new thing to happen in cryo-EM hardware beyond things that have been around for decades now,” says David Agard, founding scientific director of imaging at the biotechnology organization Biohub in Redwood City, California, who is an author of the preprint.

‘This will never work’

Cryo-EM involves freezing proteins in a thin layer of glassy ice and imaging them with an electron microscope. Biological materials generally do not absorb electrons, which makes them difficult to image directly. But they do scatter them, and this scattering alters a property of the electron wave known as the phase. Researchers have developed strategies to detect the resulting ‘phase shift’ by boosting the ‘phase contrast’, thereby enabling clearer protein imaging.

In 2010, biophysicist Robert Glaeser and physicist Holger Müller at the University of California, Berkeley, teamed up to explore the idea of focusing an intense laser onto the electron beam used for cryo-EM imaging to selectively alter the phase of non-scattered ‘background’ electrons3. This would make it possible to increase the phase contrast of these electrons relative to those that have been scattered after encountering proteins.

Cryo-electron microscopy wins chemistry Nobel

They published a relatively rudimentary proof-of-concept prototype in 20194. This work caught the attention of Biohub, which has funded subsequent development. Müller recalls attending a meeting of cryo-EM specialists, hosted by the organization during the early days of the project, where he encountered considerable enthusiasm — but also deep scepticism. “The sentiment was, ‘This would be so cool, but this will never work,’” he says.

Indeed, LPP development posed numerous technical challenges. “The laser focus that we have to generate is the brightest continuous laser focus anywhere,” says Müller. “And lasers of the required intensity didn’t exist when we started.” As a solution, he and his colleagues used precisely machined mirrors to reflect an incoming laser back and forth thousands of times, thereby amplifying it to the necessary intensity. These mirrors must be incredibly smooth — with a surface roughness of less than the diameter of a hydrogen atom — and are made of a ceramic glass that can resist damage from the intense laser beam, with further design features to ensure long-term stability.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in cryo-electron microscopy that improves the imaging quality of protein structures, enabling broader and more detailed biological research. This advancement has broad implications for structural biology and related health sciences, representing a meaningful and well-detailed achievement with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: New laser technology improves imaging of protein structures
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists use a method called cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) to take detailed pictures of tiny proteins by freezing them and using electrons to create images. However, these images can be hard to see clearly because proteins don’t absorb electrons well.

**What happened** – Two research teams developed a new tool called a laser phase plate (LPP) that helps make these images clearer. This tool uses a very powerful laser to change the way electrons behave, boosting the contrast in the pictures. After many years of work and solving tough problems, the teams showed that LPPs can improve images of small proteins in recent studies published in June 2026.

**Impact** – This new technology is important because it allows scientists to study a wider variety of proteins with better detail than before. It also makes it easier to examine proteins in their natural environment inside cells. This breakthrough could help researchers understand how proteins work and lead to advances in biology and medicine.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to improve the laser phase plate tools and use them in more cryo-EM machines around the world. This could speed up discoveries about proteins and how they affect health and disease.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A powerful laser tool now helps scientists take clearer pictures of tiny proteins, expanding what we can learn about life at the smallest level.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle technologie laser améliore l’imagerie des structures protéiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent une méthode appelée cryo-microscopie électronique (cryo-EM) pour prendre des images détaillées de très petites protéines en les congelant et en utilisant des électrons. Mais ces images sont souvent difficiles à voir clairement car les protéines n’absorbent pas bien les électrons.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Deux équipes de recherche ont développé un nouvel outil appelé plaque de phase laser (LPP) qui rend ces images plus nettes. Cet outil utilise un laser très puissant pour modifier le comportement des électrons et augmenter le contraste des images. Après de nombreuses années de travail et de résolution de problèmes difficiles, les équipes ont montré en juin 2026 que les LPP peuvent améliorer les images des petites protéines.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle technologie est importante car elle permet aux scientifiques d’étudier une plus grande variété de protéines avec plus de détails qu’avant. Elle facilite aussi l’étude des protéines dans leur environnement naturel à l’intérieur des cellules. Cette avancée pourrait aider à mieux comprendre le fonctionnement des protéines et faire progresser la biologie et la médecine.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer les plaques de phase laser et à les utiliser dans plus de microscopes cryo-EM dans le monde. Cela pourrait accélérer les découvertes sur les protéines et leur rôle dans la santé et les maladies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un outil laser puissant aide désormais les scientifiques à obtenir des images plus nettes de petites protéines, élargissant notre compréhension de la vie au plus petit niveau.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva tecnología láser mejora la imagen de estructuras proteicas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos usan un método llamado criomicroscopía electrónica (cryo-EM) para tomar imágenes detalladas de proteínas muy pequeñas congelándolas y usando electrones. Sin embargo, estas imágenes suelen ser difíciles de ver claramente porque las proteínas no absorben bien los electrones.

**Qué pasó** – Dos equipos de investigación desarrollaron una nueva herramienta llamada placa de fase láser (LPP) que ayuda a que estas imágenes sean más claras. Esta herramienta usa un láser muy potente para cambiar el comportamiento de los electrones y aumentar el contraste en las imágenes. Después de muchos años de trabajo y superar problemas difíciles, los equipos demostraron en junio de 2026 que las LPP pueden mejorar las imágenes de proteínas pequeñas.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva tecnología es importante porque permite a los científicos estudiar una mayor variedad de proteínas con más detalle que antes. También facilita examinar las proteínas en su ambiente natural dentro de las células. Este avance podría ayudar a entender mejor cómo funcionan las proteínas y avanzar en biología y medicina.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán mejorando las placas de fase láser y usándolas en más máquinas de cryo-EM en todo el mundo. Esto podría acelerar los descubrimientos sobre proteínas y su relación con la salud y las enfermedades.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una herramienta láser poderosa ahora ayuda a los científicos a tomar imágenes más claras de proteínas diminutas, ampliando lo que podemos aprender sobre la vida a nivel muy pequeño.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a finely crafted, glowing laser beam gently weaving through a smooth, reflective ceramic glass cavity shaped like an abstract microscope chamber, surrounded by delicate, translucent protein structures frozen in glassy ice, all rendered in soft natural tones of blues, whites, and warm amber light.

articles.title: First working nuclear clock heralds a new era in timekeeping
articles.summary: A clock based on radioactive thorium atoms realises a long-held ambition, demonstrating a technology that could eventually beat the accuracy of today’s best atomic clocks
articles.content:
Conceptual illustration of a clock based on atomic nuclei Oliver Diekmann, TU Wien

Scientists have built the first working nuclear clock, which uses the vibrations of atomic nuclei to keep time. Nuclear clocks have been sought after for more than two decades and could eventually allow for extraordinarily precise timekeeping and experiments that hunt for new physics.

The most accurate atomic clocks we currently have use electrons for timekeeping. Electrons occupy distinct energy levels, or orbits, around the atomic nucleus, and they only move between orbits when they are excited with light of a very specific frequency. The frequency of a light wave is defined by how many waves go past within a certain time, so counting these waves can be used for timekeeping, much like the ticking pendulum of a grandfather clock.

In an atomic clock, a laser tuned to the electron-exciting nuclear frequency is used to stimulate a collection of atoms. If it deviates from the correct frequency, fewer electrons will jump between energy levels and the frequency is corrected. This maintains the accuracy of the timekeeping, ensuring that clocks built in this way lose only a few seconds every billion years.

Atomic nuclei can also be made to bounce between energy levels. In theory, they promise greater accuracy than electrons because they have much higher energies and require more precise excitation. This means they have the potential to work to stabilities of seconds over hundreds of billions of years, far older than the age of the universe, which would help physicists look for exotic new physics.

A practical barrier to building these nuclear clocks, however, is that most nuclei require more energy to be excited than even the most energetic lasers can provide. But radioactive thorium can be excited with relatively little energy, so it has been the focus of a possible nuclear clock ever since the specific laser frequency required to excite its nucleus was first discovered in 2023.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Now, Thorsten Schumm at the Vienna University of Technology in Austria and his colleagues have built such a device. Made from thorium, it is already showing promise in the hunt for elusive dark matter particles. “It’s the culmination of 15 to 20 years of research,” says Schumm. “It’s amazing. Very few researchers actually see their dream become true.”

Previous systems have shown thorium’s nuclear frequency can be excited by the right laser, but they lacked the distinct frequency adjustment mechanism of a working clock. “If there’s ever going to be a ‘this is it’ moment, it’s probably this,” says Harry Morgan at the University of Manchester, UK.

Schumm and his colleagues built the clock by embedding the thorium in a crystal made from calcium fluoride and shining an ultraviolet laser through it. The laser, which acts as the clock’s ticking, periodically switches between two frequencies just above and below thorium’s known nuclear frequency. If the slightly higher and lower frequencies are absorbed by thorium equally, then the laser is tuned correctly. If it is different, then the clock uses this as feedback to tune the laser to the right frequency.

The nuclear clock doesn’t yet have the stability of the best atomic clocks, instead running at tens of seconds lost every billion years. But Schumm and his team say the clock is more of a proof of principle and they have yet to fine-tune the system with the best available lasers and electronics.

For such a simple prototype, it is demonstrating impressive stability, says team member Ekkehard Peik at PTB, the German national metrology institute. “What impressed me the most was that the system ran overnight and for 24 hours without user intervention,” he says. “This is something that has not been achieved so rapidly with other optical clocks.”

But even without this stability, the nuclear clock can do things that atomic clocks can’t. Because the nucleus is shielded from the chaotic electromagnetic environment of the atom’s electrons, it has a very precise transition that isn’t affected by moving electrons, which makes it more sensitive to external effects of physics. In practice, this means sensitive properties of the nucleons can be measured without the noise of electrons, which helps both for more accurate “ticking” and for measuring fundamental physical properties with greater precision.

. It also doesn’t need to be cooled to extremely low temperatures or have its atoms placed in a vacuum like atomic clocks, and instead works at room temperature. “It’s really the most simple thing you can imagine,” says Schumm.

This means the system should be more easily miniaturised, he says, which could allow it to be deployed in many different kinds of experiments, such as satellite tests of relativity. “While the current performance is considerably below the current state of the art, we can expect orders-of-magnitude improvement in the near future,” says Eric Hudson at the University of California, Los Angeles.

Schumm and his colleagues used the very high energies of the thorium nucleus to rule out possible dark matter particles. If dark matter is an electromagnetic-like force that permeates our universe, then it should subtly change the nuclear energy transitions of all matter, including thorium. This would make the specific frequency that the clock works at measurably different, which would be obvious because of thorium’s high nuclear frequency. “It’s a little bit like if you want to measure the change of length [in a metal] because of heat temperature change,” says Schumm. “The longer your stick, the larger the effect.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports the successful creation of the first working nuclear clock, a significant technological breakthrough with the potential for extraordinarily precise timekeeping. This advancement has broad implications for physics research, including the hunt for dark matter and improved fundamental measurements. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a major scientific achievement with potential large-scale benefits for science and technology.
articles.personality_title: Scientists build first working nuclear clock using thorium atoms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been trying for over 20 years to create a nuclear clock, a device that uses the vibrations of atomic nuclei to keep time. Current atomic clocks use electrons moving between energy levels to measure time with great accuracy.

**What happened** – Researchers at Vienna University of Technology in Austria have built the first working nuclear clock. This clock uses radioactive thorium atoms placed inside a crystal and shines an ultraviolet laser on them. The laser adjusts its frequency to match the thorium nuclei’s energy changes, creating a precise ticking mechanism.

**Impact** – This clock is a big step forward because nuclear vibrations can be more stable than electron vibrations. Even though the new clock is not yet as precise as the best atomic clocks, it can work at room temperature and does not need extreme cooling or vacuum. It also can measure tiny changes in physics better than current clocks. The clock has already helped scientists rule out some possible dark matter particles.

**What's next step** – The team plans to improve the clock’s stability by using better lasers and electronics. They also hope to make the device smaller and easier to use in different experiments, such as testing Einstein’s theories in space.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have created the first nuclear clock using thorium atoms, opening new possibilities for ultra-precise timekeeping and physics research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques créent la première horloge nucléaire fonctionnelle avec des atomes de thorium
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis plus de 20 ans, les scientifiques tentent de créer une horloge nucléaire, un appareil qui utilise les vibrations des noyaux atomiques pour mesurer le temps. Les horloges atomiques actuelles utilisent les électrons qui changent de niveau d'énergie pour garder un temps très précis.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Technologie de Vienne en Autriche ont construit la première horloge nucléaire fonctionnelle. Cette horloge utilise des atomes radioactifs de thorium placés dans un cristal et un laser ultraviolet qui ajuste sa fréquence pour suivre les changements d’énergie des noyaux de thorium, créant ainsi un mécanisme de tic-tac précis.

**Impact** – Cette horloge est une avancée importante car les vibrations nucléaires peuvent être plus stables que celles des électrons. Même si cette nouvelle horloge n’est pas encore aussi précise que les meilleures horloges atomiques, elle fonctionne à température ambiante sans besoin de refroidissement extrême ou de vide. Elle peut aussi mesurer de très petits changements physiques mieux que les horloges actuelles. Elle a déjà aidé à exclure certaines particules possibles de la matière noire.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit d’améliorer la stabilité de l’horloge en utilisant de meilleurs lasers et composants électroniques. Ils espèrent aussi rendre l’appareil plus petit et facile à utiliser dans différentes expériences, comme tester les théories d’Einstein dans l’espace.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont créé la première horloge nucléaire avec des atomes de thorium, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour une mesure du temps ultra-précise et la recherche en physique.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean el primer reloj nuclear funcional con átomos de torio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante más de 20 años, los científicos han intentado crear un reloj nuclear, un dispositivo que usa las vibraciones de los núcleos atómicos para medir el tiempo. Los relojes atómicos actuales usan electrones que cambian de nivel energético para mantener un tiempo muy preciso.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Tecnológica de Viena en Austria construyeron el primer reloj nuclear funcional. Este reloj usa átomos radiactivos de torio dentro de un cristal y un láser ultravioleta que ajusta su frecuencia para seguir los cambios energéticos de los núcleos de torio, creando un mecanismo de tic-tac preciso.

**Impacto** – Este reloj es un gran avance porque las vibraciones nucleares pueden ser más estables que las de los electrones. Aunque el nuevo reloj aún no es tan preciso como los mejores relojes atómicos, funciona a temperatura ambiente sin necesidad de enfriamiento extremo o vacío. También puede medir cambios físicos muy pequeños mejor que los relojes actuales. Ya ayudó a descartar algunas posibles partículas de materia oscura.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea mejorar la estabilidad del reloj usando mejores láseres y electrónica. También esperan hacer el dispositivo más pequeño y fácil de usar en diferentes experimentos, como pruebas de las teorías de Einstein en el espacio.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos crearon el primer reloj nuclear con átomos de torio, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para la medición ultra precisa del tiempo y la investigación en física.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing thorium atom’s nucleus at the center of a delicate crystalline lattice, with subtle waves of light symbolizing precise laser frequencies gently oscillating around it, set against a soft, abstract background evoking the vastness of time and space.
